label: Bit Gold (Nick Szabo, 2005)
brief:
Nick Szabo described Bit Gold in essays starting around 1998 and in a more-developed 2005 blog post. The proposal is a digital scarcity built on chained proof-of-work: a participant takes a public "challenge string" derived from the most recent solved puzzle, finds an input whose hash satisfies a difficulty target, registers the solution in a property-title registry, and the chain of solutions becomes a record of cumulative work that nobody can rewrite cheaply [1]. The framing — "unforgeably costly bits" — is Szabo's, and it is the cleanest one-line statement of what proof-of-work-backed money is actually trying to be.

The pieces Bit Gold got right and Bitcoin shares: chaining the proofs so that each one builds on the last, making the cost of rewriting history compound; using the hash output itself as the unit of value; not requiring a trusted issuer to mint or to validate; recognizing that the scarcity of the resulting tokens depends on the cost of producing them, not on any institutional commitment [2]. The piece Bit Gold left unsolved: the property-title registry. Szabo proposed a Byzantine-agreement quorum of independent servers as the registry layer, but acknowledged that designing the quorum, gating membership, and incentivizing honest behavior were open problems.

Szabo did not publish the 2005 essay in a way the Bitcoin whitepaper could cite by 2008, which is why Bit Gold is not in the references list — but the conceptual ancestry is direct enough that some readers, on reading the whitepaper, initially suspected Szabo of being Satoshi (Szabo has consistently denied this) [3]. Bit Gold is the proposal that frames the problem most cleanly; Bitcoin is the system that solved the registry-and-incentive layer Bit Gold could not.

sources.label: Nick Szabo — Bit Gold (2005)
sources.excerpt: What we want is a protocol whereby unforgeably costly bits could be created online with minimal dependence on trusted third parties, and then securely stored, transferred, and assayed with similar minimal trust. A long string of computational work, with each new bit-gold building on the last, would give us this: scarcity that is verifiable by inspection.

sources.label: Nick Szabo — Shelling Out: The Origins of Money (2002)
sources.excerpt: Collectibles are precursors to money: durable, scarce, hard to counterfeit, recognizable. Bit Gold attempts to extend this lineage into the digital realm by producing tokens whose scarcity is enforced by computation rather than by physical or institutional barriers.

sources.label: Nathaniel Popper — Decoding the Enigma of Satoshi Nakamoto (NYT, 2015)
sources.excerpt: Szabo had described Bit Gold in essays beginning in 1998 and in a more developed form in 2005. He has consistently denied being Satoshi Nakamoto. The conceptual lineage from Bit Gold to Bitcoin is unmistakable; the engineering work that turned the concept into a running system was Satoshi's.
